title: Self-Supervised Discovery of Neural Circuits in Spatially Patterned Neural Responses with Graph Neural Networks
abstract: Inferring synaptic connectivity from neural population activity is a fundamental challenge in computational neuroscience, complicated by partial observability and mismatches between inference models and true circuit dynamics. In this study, we propose a graph-based neural inference model that simultaneously predicts neural activity and infers latent connectivity by modeling neurons as interacting nodes in a graph. The architecture features two distinct modules: one for learning structural connectivity and another for predicting future spiking activity via a graph neural network (GNN). Our model accommodates unobserved neurons through auxiliary nodes, allowing for inference in partially observed circuits. We evaluate this approach using synthetic data generated from ring attractor network models and real spike recordings from head direction cells in mice. Across a wide range of conditions, including varying recurrent connectivity, external inputs, and incomplete observations, our model reliably resolves spurious correlations and recovers accurate weight profiles. When applied to real data, the inferred connectivity aligns with theoretical predictions of continuous attractor models. These results highlight the potential of GNN-based models to infer latent neural circuitry through self-supervised structure learning, while leveraging the spike prediction task to flexibly link connectivity and dynamics across both simulated and biological neural systems.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The authors contribute to the question of computational neuroscience of how connectivity can be extracted from spiking data. They focus on two datasets. The first one is a simplified variant of the ring-model that was previously used by Das and Fiete (as cited). The second data set consists of electrophysiological recordings from head direction cells.

The  paper's main contribution is to address a well-known and acknowledged issue on connectivity estimation: the overestimation of connection in the presence of  strong correlation in activity (Das and Fiete 2020).

The proposed method of the authors uses a message-passing approach of graph neural networks and gated recurrent units. It works well on the artificial data (ring model of Das and Fiete).

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
The artificial data set was designed to contain strong correlations that not directly reflected in the weights. Based on the artificial data, the method proposed in the manuscript does seem to work. The problem it addresses is arguably serious and essential and hence an important contribution. This is clearly a strength of the paper and the title makes clear that this is also the main focus.

Unfortunately, the case with hidden neurons does not seem to work very well which reduces the importance of the approach.

The results on the real experimental dataset  (head direction cells) do not offer any new insights. Moreover, I am not sure if the real dataset  is suitable to back up the main claims.

The specific artificial data set has inherent weaknesses.  In contrast to a real spiking model (e.g., nonlinear Hawkes process with strong negative self-feedback or integrate-and-fire models with reset), the neuron model used in  the artificial data has no self-feedback and is made to run in the high-noise limit (inhomogeneous Poisson process). As described, neither the  threshold-crossing model nor the LNP model have any refractoriness or reset. The authors say that they follow Das and Fiete (cited), and if this is what they do the data there is artificially made to have a mean interspike interval of 16 ms, with a broad Poisson-like interval distribution.

QUESTIONS:
1. The authors show that on the ring data,  their approach indeed 'explains away the correlations that arise from co-activated neurons'. It would be great if they could explain how their equations (3) -(6) achieve this.

2. The improvement over GLM in the weakly correlated case is not  impressive. Moreover, I had (may be wrongly) the impression that the authors injected inputs by changing the recurrent weights. This unusual procedure may put the GLM method at a disadvantage. How does the performance of the proposed method compare to GLM in a more classic setup when the external inputs are injected directly into neurons, for fixed weight matrix?

3. As an additional control for the GLM fit: If you initialized the GLM method with the correct weight matrix (and use the procedure under point 2), would  the GLM fitting procedure (as a minimal condition) stay at the solution or still move away from the correct solution during fitting. ?

4. How would the procedure work on more realistic spiking neural networks. In general, the model of a ring-attractor is of rather limited generality because of its rotation symmetry. No real data ever will have these symmetries. Can you test the method also on some more realistic spiking neural network?

5. Line 129, what is the origin of the symmetry of the weight matrix? In Fig 5, is N_0=21 or 19 correct?

6. How does a simulation of the final extracted model in the ABSENCE OF INPUT look like? Can you ensure that there is no run-away activity? Do bumps form spontaneously?

7.  I wonder if the authors could test on any grid cell recordings, which is closer to the multiple bump setup than the chosen head direction data set.

8. The choice of comparison points/alternative methods is somewhat arbitrary and limited.
Alternative methods: The authors may want to also consider  work of Felipe Gerhard [1,2],  Guillaume Bellec [3], or Schimel [4[

[1] F. Gerhard et al., (2013) Successful Reconstruction of a Physiological Circuit with Known Connectivity from Spiking Activity Alone; PLOS Comput. Biol. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1003138

[2] F. Gerhard et al. On the stability and dynamics of stochastic spiking neuron models: Nonlinear Hawkes process and point process GLMs, PLoS computational biology 13 e1005390 (2017)

[3] G. Bellec et al. (2021)  Fitting summary statistics of neural data with a differentiable spiking network simulator. Neural Information Processing Systems 34 (NeurIPS 2021)

[4] M Schimel et al., (2021) iLQR-VAE: control-based learning of input-driven dynamics with applications to neural data], bioRxiv, 2021.10. 07.463540

LIMITATIONS:
no issues
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper addresses the interesting question of how to infer synaptic connectivity from neural population activity. The authors develop a graph neural network (GNN)-based model that predicts both latent connectivity and neural activity by designing two functionally specialized network modules. As a result, they demonstrate consistently strong performance across diverse conditions on both synthetic and real datasets. Overall, although I am not an expert in GNNs, I find this paper easy to read and well supported by extensive results (i.e., various types of datasets and generative conditions).

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
+ The writing is clear and well supported by extensive results.

+ The paper addresses interesting research questions and demonstrates strong performance.

- I did not identify any significant weaknesses.

QUESTIONS:
- In real-world recordings, neural activity is easily affected by various sources of noise. It would be helpful for the authors to further explain or validate the robustness of the proposed method under such conditions.

- In Tables 1 and 2, what is the magnitude of the standard deviation for the listed models?

LIMITATIONS:
- In real-world recordings, neural activity is easily affected by various sources of noise. It would be helpful for the authors to further explain or validate the robustness of the proposed method under such conditions.

- As partially discussed by the authors, one main limitation is the assumption that latent connectivity is fixed and follows a one-to-one mapping (i.e., each neuron pair has only one connection). It would be beneficial to discuss how the proposed method could be adapted to scenarios involving synaptic plasticity or more complex mapping relationships.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper introduces a self-supervised approach for inferring neural circuit connectivity from population spike activity using graph neural networks (GNNs). The model consists of two main modules: one for learning structural connectivity and another for predicting future spiking activity. With such design, the model can be applied to infer structural connectivity even under partially observed circuits. The method is evaluated on both synthetic data and real spiking recordings.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
## Strength:

It is interesting that the introduced method can be applied to partially observed neural circuit. The task is challenge but is existing for the neural recordings. 

Authors provide thorough discussion on different circuit scenario from fully observed network, weakly corrected fully observed, weakly correlated and partially observed.

## Weakness 
Connectivity pattern with local Mexican-hat profile is used for synthetic data generation, it would be good to discuss how the performance change with different connectivity patters. 

Related work discussion is not completed, for example how is the method comparable to the 

[1] NetFormer: https://openreview.net/pdf?id=bcTjW5kS4W

[2] AMAG: https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/1c70ba3591d0694a535089e1c25888d7-Paper-Conference.pdf

QUESTIONS:
The current work focuses on ring attractor networks. How might the proposed GNN architecture and self-supervised learning objective need to be adapted or extended to infer connectivity in neural circuits with fundamentally different topological structures or functional properties?

For the real HD neural, how the parameter $N$, $N_o$, $N_h$ are decided, especially is there any general instruction for different real neural datasets?

How the proposed method can be generalized to large scale neural activities? What is the computation cost, and inference cost,

LIMITATIONS:
Yes.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper addresses the important challenge of estimating synaptic connectivity from neural population activity. The authors propose a GNN-based approach that simultaneously predicts neural activity and infers connectivity given past observed neural activity and stimuli as inputs. The proposed model has two components: (i) a structure learning module -- an MLP that estimates pairwise connectivity from the embeddings of a pair of neurons' activities, and (ii) a spike prediction module that is a GNN, where each node corresponds to a neuron, and uses message passing to compute node embeddings. The message passing operations of the GNN, specifically the aggregation step, use the pairwise connectivity computed by the structure learning module. These node embeddings are then decoded into log firing rates. The entire network is trained by minimizing the Poisson negative log-likelihood of the observed neural activity. The proposed approach is first evaluated on simulated data from ring attractor networks. The results demonstrate that the model outperforms baselines at both predicting neural activity and inferring the connectivity across a range of conditions, including varying recurrent connectivity, external inputs, and incomplete observations. The authors also show that this framework can effectively recover key features of the underlying circuit in real neural data, which is promising.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
This paper proposes a novel approach for inferring synaptic connectivity from neural population activity, an important challenge in computational neuroscience. The authors demonstrate that their method successfully predicts neural activity and connectivity in simulated networks, outperforming existing baselines. Importantly, they also show the method's potential by applying it to real neural recordings. They also propose interesting future directions, such as applying the model to grid cell data.

The authors also discuss a few limitations of their work: (i) the model currently assumes that the network connectivity is static, and (ii) different underlying circuit configurations can produce similar neural dynamics, thus the model can correctly predict neural activity while inferring connectivity that is different from the ground truth. Another interesting question is the model's scalability. The GNN in the spike prediction module assigns one node per neuron. How efficient would this approach be for larger systems with thousands of neurons or more, particularly under conditions of partial observability where only a few hundred neurons are recorded?

Overall, the paper is very clearly presented. Minor suggestions for improvement would be to add color bars to the weight matrix heatmaps and add axis labels to the few panels missing them.

QUESTIONS:
- Does $f_{out}$ in eq 1 include a nonlinearity?

- The results reported in tables 1, 2 and 3 are averaged over multiple trials. Could you clarify what the trials refer to? Are these multiple trials from the test split? Or do they correspond to seeds for training the network? 

- In Fig 3C, the error bars are wider in the bottom row compared to the top. Why is that the case?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
This paper proposes a self-supervised framework that jointly predicts spikes and infers latent synaptic connectivity with a graph neural network, using a dedicated structure-learning block coupled to a message-passing predictor. The model handles partial observability via auxiliary nodes and is evaluated on synthetic ring-attractor circuits and real head-direction recordings, showing improved spike prediction and more faithful recovery of weight profiles relative to standard baselines.

The paper is clearly written and technically sound, with a simple architecture that addresses correlation-driven artifacts in connectivity inference. Reviewers raised fair questions about scalability, generalization beyond ring geometries, the distinction from GLMs, and robustness under partial observability; the authors provided clarifications, added controls, and strengthened the discussion without introducing new risks or unresolved contradictions. With all reviews leaning to accept, the AC also agrees that the manuscript meets the bar for clarity, substance, and empirical support.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
